content:
How the Iran conflict exposed the UAE’s deeper dependence on U.S. security, dollars, AI chips, and capital markets.

11 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image made in CanvaThere is a strange thing that happens during a crisis.
You expect countries to pull away from danger. You expect them to hedge. You expect them to question their alliances, soften their positions, and look for a way out.
But sometimes the opposite happens.
Sometimes a crisis does not weaken a relationship. It reveals why that relationship existed in the first place.
That is what I think we are seeing with the United Arab Emirates and the United States.
After the war with Iran put pressure on the Gulf, the obvious assumption would have been that Abu Dhabi would start creating distance from Washington. The UAE is not some isolated military outpost. It is a business hub. It is a financial centre. It is an aviation hub. It is a place that sells itself to the world as safe, stable, polished, and open for business.
So when a regional war starts threatening that image, the natural question is simple.
Why stay so close to the United States if that closeness makes you a target?
But that question misses the bigger picture.
The UAE is not moving closer to America because it enjoys risk. It is moving closer because the risk has made one thing very clear: in the modern Gulf, security, capital, technology, and currency are all connected.
And America sits at the centre of all four.
That is the real story here.
Not oil.
Not diplomacy.
Not even just Iran.
The real story is that the UAE is trying to secure its place in the next version of global power. And for now, that version still runs through Washington.
The old Gulf bargain is changing
For decades, the Gulf’s relationship with America was easy to explain.
The Gulf had oil. America had military power. The Gulf supplied energy to the global economy, often priced in dollars, and America helped provide the security umbrella that kept the system running.
That was the rough bargain.
It was never as simple or as clean as people make it sound, but the basic logic was clear enough. Oil, dollars, military protection, shipping lanes, bases, weapons, and political alignment all lived inside the same system.
But the UAE is not trying to be just another oil state.
That is the whole point.
Dubai did not become Dubai by acting like a normal oil economy. Abu Dhabi did not build its sovereign wealth strategy just to sit on hydrocarbons forever. The UAE has spent years trying to become something more durable: a logistics hub, a finance hub, a tourism hub, an aviation hub, and now, very clearly, a technology hub.
This is where the relationship with America becomes more interesting.
Because the old bargain was about oil.
The new bargain is about artificial intelligence.
That might sound dramatic, but I do not think it is exaggerated. The next generation of power will not only be decided by who has the most oil reserves. It will be decided by who has access to advanced chips, cloud infrastructure, data centres, energy capacity, capital markets, and military protection.
The UAE wants to be inside that system.
Not watching it from the outside.
Iran exposed the UAE’s vulnerability
The UAE has built one of the most impressive national brands in the world.
When people think of Dubai and Abu Dhabi, they think of skyscrapers, airports, luxury hotels, ports, conferences, finance, and global money. The image is stability. The image is control. The image is a country that can sit in a chaotic region and somehow remain above the chaos.
But war has a way of ruining branding.
Once Iran started putting pressure on Gulf states connected to the U.S. and Israel, the UAE’s position became more exposed. It hosts major American business interests. It is tied to Washington’s security network. It normalised relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords. It sits in one of the most strategically sensitive parts of the world.
That makes it valuable.
But it also makes it vulnerable.
And this is the part that matters: Iran did not need to destroy the UAE to hurt it. It only needed to make investors, tourists, airlines, banks, and companies question whether the country was still as insulated from regional conflict as everyone assumed.
For a place like the UAE, perception is not a side issue. It is part of the economy.
Confidence is an asset.
When markets fall, flights get disrupted, infrastructure becomes a concern, or global headlines start connecting Dubai and Abu Dhabi with war risk, the damage is not only physical. It is reputational.
That is why the UAE’s response is so important.
It could have interpreted the crisis as a warning to pull back from the United States.
Instead, it seems to have read the crisis as proof that it needs the United States even more.
Defense is the foundation
The defense relationship between the UAE and the U.S. is not new.
This is not some sudden partnership created overnight because of one war. The relationship has been developing for decades, especially since the Gulf War. The UAE has hosted important American military assets, worked with the U.S. on regional security, and bought large amounts of American military equipment.
There is a reason the UAE has often been described as one of Washington’s most capable partners in the region.
But the Iran conflict has made the value of that partnership much clearer.
For the UAE, American defense support is not just about prestige. It is practical. It is about air defense, intelligence, missile interception, maritime security, drones, radar systems, military training, and access to advanced weapons.
That matters because the UAE is small, rich, and exposed.
It does not have the strategic depth of a large country. It cannot simply absorb endless attacks and carry on. Its economy is built around being open. It needs airports to function. It needs shipping lanes to remain safe. It needs foreign companies to believe their staff and capital are secure.
So when Iran shows that it can create pressure across the Gulf, the UAE’s need for a serious security umbrella becomes impossible to ignore.
And for America, the relationship is just as useful.
The U.S. gets a reliable Gulf partner, access, basing advantages, port access, regional influence, and a way to project power near some of the world’s most important shipping routes.
This is not charity.
It is a transaction.
The UAE gets protection and technology. America gets position and influence.
That is how power works.
The UAE is buying more than weapons
It is easy to look at U.S.-UAE arms deals and see only military hardware.
Missiles. Drones. Defense systems. Aircraft. Radars.
But weapons are only one layer of the relationship.
When a country buys advanced military systems from the United States, it is also buying into a long-term ecosystem. Training, maintenance, software, parts, upgrades, interoperability, doctrine, and political trust all come with the package.
You do not just buy the weapon.
You buy the relationship around the weapon.
That is why defense deals matter so much. They create dependency, but they also create alignment. They make it harder for both sides to walk away from each other.
For the UAE, that is useful because it strengthens its security position.
For the U.S., it is useful because it keeps the UAE inside the American orbit at a time when China is trying to expand its influence across the Middle East.
This is where the story starts to move beyond Iran.
Iran is the immediate pressure.
China is the bigger strategic backdrop.
The AI layer changes everything
The most important part of the U.S.-UAE relationship may no longer be oil or weapons.
It may be artificial intelligence.
The UAE has made it very clear that it wants to become a serious AI power. That means data centres, advanced chips, energy supply, cloud infrastructure, sovereign investment, and partnerships with major American technology companies.
But there is a problem.
The best AI chips are not neutral products anymore. They are geopolitical assets.
Access to advanced Nvidia chips is now part of the U.S.-China power struggle. Washington does not want sensitive technology flowing into Chinese hands. So if the UAE wants deep access to American AI technology, it has to convince the U.S. that it is a trusted partner.
That is where companies like G42 become important.
The UAE wants to build its AI future with access to American chips and American cloud partners. But that access comes with conditions. The U.S. wants assurances that Chinese technology will not sit inside sensitive parts of the stack.
In plain English, the UAE is being asked to choose.
Not completely. Not loudly. Not in a dramatic Cold War speech.
But structurally.
If Abu Dhabi wants the highest level of American AI access, it cannot look too close to Beijing at the same time.
That is the real meaning of the AI partnership.
It is not just business.
It is alignment.
And that is why I think the old Gulf bargain is being replaced by something new.
The first version was oil for security.
The new version is capital and AI alignment for security, chips, and access to the American system.
Capital is the quiet weapon
The UAE is not just a buyer in this relationship.
It is also a major investor.
That is what makes the partnership different from a simple dependency story. The UAE is not just sitting there waiting for America to protect it. It is using its capital to buy influence, build relevance, and position itself inside the industries that will define the next decade.
Energy.
Manufacturing.
Semiconductors.
AI infrastructure.
Data centres.
Private credit.
LNG.
American equities.
Corporate bonds.
This is not random diversification. It is strategic placement.
The UAE understands that if the future is going to be built around AI infrastructure, then capital will matter almost as much as technology. The companies building this future need huge amounts of money, huge amounts of energy, and huge amounts of physical infrastructure.
The UAE has money.
It has energy.
It wants relevance.
America has the innovation ecosystem, the capital markets, the technology companies, and the reserve currency.
That is a powerful combination.
So when the UAE commits to massive long-term investment in the United States, it is not just looking for returns. It is buying a deeper seat at the table.
This is how modern influence works.
Not just through flags and speeches, but through balance sheets.
The dollar still matters
One of the most interesting parts of this whole story is the UAE’s reported interest in a dollar swap line or financial backstop from the United States.
At first, that sounds strange.
Why would a wealthy Gulf state with major sovereign wealth need a dollar lifeline?
But I do not think the point is only emergency liquidity.
It is also about status, trust, and access.
A dollar swap line is not just a financial tool. It is a signal. It says: this country is inside the trusted part of the dollar system.
That matters because the UAE’s economy is deeply connected to global finance. Its currency is pegged to the dollar. Its banks, trade flows, investment vehicles, and financial reputation all depend on confidence.
If war creates stress, access to dollars becomes more than a technical issue. It becomes a strategic issue.
For the UAE, being close to the dollar system protects its financial model.
For the U.S., keeping the UAE close protects the dollar’s role in the Gulf.
Again, this is not charity.
It is mutual dependence.
The UAE wants the security of the dollar. America wants key Gulf partners to keep choosing the dollar.
That is the quiet financial layer beneath the military and technology story.
This is not loyalty. It is strategy.
I think it would be a mistake to describe the UAE’s closeness to America as simple loyalty.
Countries do not behave like friends in a group chat. They behave according to interests.
The UAE is not moving closer to
